narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your lower-middle family income background, and we appreciate the strength that brings to your journey. The admissions model evaluated your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 44, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2, and your plan to attend full-time—against patterns from thousands of past students who took the bar exam. It predicted a 48% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which is high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application to prioritize students with stronger signals of bar passage success. While no one can predict the future perfectly, this forecast means the school sees elevated risk in your profile compared to applicants likely to pass. Your standout LSAT score was the biggest positive force, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass, well above the typical score in the dataset. However, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest negative pull, signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar failure compared to peers in higher deciles. Your Year 1 decile also worked against you, contributing notably to the failure prediction by being lower than average. On a brighter note, your family income and full-time enrollment status each nudged things slightly toward success, aligning with common patterns among bar passers. Overall, your academics in school outweighed even your impressive LSAT, setting you apart from typical admitted students who often have higher deciles closer to 5 or 6 alongside solid test scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but a data-driven call to strengthen those school performance areas. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your deciles, perhaps seeking mentorship tailored to students from similar backgrounds, and reapply with updated credentials that highlight your resilience. You've got the LSAT edge; building on that could flip the script next time. We're rooting for you.
